title: Debt as a tool — not a moral failure
subtitle: The question about debt is never whether it is good or bad. It is whether it is cheap or expensive, productive or consumptive, manageable or excessive.

body.content: Debt carries significant moral weight in most cultures. It is associated with failure, weakness, or poor discipline. This framing is counterproductive and imprecise. Debt is a financial instrument — a mechanism for accessing capital today in exchange for a future repayment obligation. Like any tool, its value depends entirely on how and why it is used.

body.label: Productive vs. consumptive debt
body.content: Productive debt finances assets that generate returns exceeding the cost of borrowing: a mortgage on a property that appreciates, a business loan that funds growth, a student loan that increases earning capacity. Consumptive debt finances spending that generates no return: a credit card balance for discretionary purchases, a loan for a depreciating asset. The distinction is not about the lender or the product — it is about what the capital is used for.

The cost of debt is its interest rate. The benefit of debt is the return generated by the assets it finances. When the return exceeds the cost, debt creates value. When the cost exceeds the return, debt destroys it. This arithmetic is the entirety of the leverage decision.

Large institutions — governments, corporations, financial firms — use debt routinely and deliberately because they understand this arithmetic. Individual investors who reflexively avoid all debt, or who carry high-cost consumer debt while holding low-yield savings accounts, are allowing a cultural bias to override basic financial logic.

Evaluate debt by its cost, its purpose, and its manageability — not by how it feels. A mortgage at 4% financing a property earning 7% is intelligent capital allocation. A credit card at 22% financing consumption is not. The tool is neutral. The use determines the outcome.
